title: Tree and DAG Taxonomies
concept: Taxonomies can be structured as simple trees with single parents, or Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) allowing multiple parent classes.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a major advantage of hierarchical classification over flat classification?

options.text: It groups classes logically, enabling better mistake handling and structured decisions.
It runs in constant time regardless of network depth.
It eliminates the need for validation data.

exercises.explain: Correct! Hierarchical structures preserve relationship information, making predictions and errors more logical.

cards.title: The platypus puzzle
cards.body: A platypus is a mammal, but it also lays eggs (like reptiles and birds). In a simple folder system, you'd have to choose: does it go under 'Mammals' or 'Egg-Layers'? But in a graph structure, it can link to both. This is the difference between Trees and DAGs.

cards.title: Trees vs. DAGs
cards.body:
Tree Taxonomy: Each class has exactly one parent class. The structure starts at a single root and branches outward.
DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) Taxonomy: A class can have multiple parent classes, allowing overlapping categories, but it contains no loops (no child can be its own ancestor).

cards.title: A river system vs. a road network
cards.body: A tree is like a river system: smaller streams flow into larger ones, but they never split and rejoin in complex loops. A DAG is like a road network: you can take different highways to reach the same city, but there are no circular loops that keep you driving in circles forever.

cards.title: ImageNet WordNet Hierarchy
cards.body: The ImageNet dataset is organized using WordNet, which is a DAG. For example, a 'Red Wine' is a child of both 'Beverage' and 'Fermented Juice'. It inherits properties from both parent branches.

cards.title: Hierarchies are not always clean trees
cards.body: You might think most things in life fit into a clean tree structure. In reality, human concepts are highly overlapping. A 'Smartphone' is both an 'Electronic Communication Device' and a 'Camera', which requires a DAG structure.

left.text:
Root -> Node A, Node B
Each node has ONE parent
Simple branching paths

right.text:
Node C has parents: A AND B
Allows multiple parent classes
No circular loops allowed

exercises.prompt: What is the key defining feature of a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) taxonomy in classification?
exercises.explain: A DAG allows nodes to have multiple parents, but prevents loops (hence 'acyclic').

options.text: A class can have multiple parent classes, but there are no loops.
Every class must have exactly one parent class.
The nodes are connected in a circle.
There are no parent-child relationships at all.

exercises.prompt: True or false: ImageNet's classes are structured in a simple tree, where no class has more than one parent.
exercises.explain: False. ImageNet is based on WordNet, which is a Directed Acyclic Graph where classes can inherit from multiple parents.
Acyclic means 'non-cyclic' or containing no loops where a node can trace back to itself.
exercises.sentence: A taxonomy structure that contains no circular loops is described as ___.

exercises.prompt: Which of these concepts represents a DAG relationship rather than a simple tree?
exercises.explain: Inheriting from two distinct parent categories (Utility Vehicles and Passenger Cars) requires a DAG.

options.text: A 'SUV' is classified under both 'Utility Vehicles' and 'Passenger Cars'
A 'Husky' is classified only under the parent class 'Dog'
A 'Golden Delicious' is classified only under 'Apple'

exercises.prompt: You are modeling a taxonomy for food items. A 'Cheese Pizza' is both a 'Baked Goods' item and a 'Dairy Product'. If you must choose between a Tree and a DAG structure, which one should you choose, and why?

options.text: DAG, because it allows 'Cheese Pizza' to link to both parent classes simultaneously.
Tree, because it forces the pizza to belong to only one folder, which is less confusing for the loss function.
Tree, because a pizza cannot be acyclic.
DAG, because it allows circular loops where pizza is its own parent.

exercises.explain: Exactly! A tree would force you to choose one parent class, losing the connection to the other. A DAG fits overlapping real-world concepts perfectly. Next, let's look at how we design loss functions that respect these structures!
